In the heart of a bustling city, nestled among towering skyscrapers and vibrant street life, stood a quaint, red-brick house with a garden that bloomed with roses and lilies. It was a sight that had been etched into the memory of a man named Thomas, who had grown up in this very house some thirty years ago.

Thomas, now a successful architect, was visiting his hometown for a reunion with old friends. The city had changed, modernized, but the little red house, untouched by time, stood as a beacon of his past. He stood before it, his heart pounding with a mix of nostalgia and disbelief.

He remembered the countless afternoons spent in the garden, the laughter echoing through the house, the warmth of his family surrounding him. He remembered the sense of security and love that this house had provided him, a sanctuary from the world outside.

With a deep breath, Thomas stepped onto the familiar cobblestone path, his shoes crunching on the gravel. The front door creaked open, just as he remembered it, and he stepped inside.

The house was exactly as he had left it. The living room, with its worn-out sofa and the wooden coffee table where he had built countless Lego creations. The kitchen, with the same chipped mug he used to drink his morning tea. The stairs leading to his old bedroom, where he spent countless nights dreaming of the future.

The velling, the no ver, silence broken, bleached echo,
In its cabled-mean or thinned from air-rehuman,
Its cerrily-bleak bearness, earthly deafness
In which no echo's lowness or the bitter thing
That verrifies it: something heard, a voiceless echo.

In the refracted, the refracted, the refracted,
By the light I am, the light I am, the light
The light I am, the light I am, the light
I am the light, I am the light, I am the light
That is my light, my light, my light, my light,

A man stands, waiting for the bus, his breath clouding
In the cold air, his feet stinging from the cold,
His hands shoved deep in his pockets, hoping
To ward off the chill. He has worked a long day,
His mind numb from the drudgery of work,
His spirit blunted by the endless repetition
Of tasks that never seem to end.

He stares blankly at the street, watching the cars
Speed by, a blur of color and noise, as if
They were the only things moving in this still world.
He longs for the bus to come, to whisk him away
From this place, from this moment, from this life
That seems to stretch out before him like an endless
Tunnel with no end in sight.

Suddenly, he hears a sound, a sound
That seems to come from nowhere and everywhere,
A sound that is both comforting and terrifying

Under the endless recurrence of stars, beneath the faceless, forlorn reflection of me, I stand.

The train glides through the night, the city's dark embrace a comforting shroud, as we travel towards an eternal now.

The world outside fades, replaced by the echoes of a thousand souls, trapped in the mirror of time.

The darkness envelops me, the wind whispers, "Welcome home, where the midnight commute is timeless."
